content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:9a707b82

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The transcript contains durable user interests/preferences, a dated prior event, concrete baking experiments/results, and a reusable summary of the sourdough shortcake/flour discussion.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        none

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<0>; type=USER; name=User; reason=First-person statements need the stable USER entity as subject and no existing candidates are available.
- mention=strawberries; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=PRODUCT; name=Strawberries; reason=Reusable food ingredient discussed as something the user is interested in and buys/snacks on.
- mention=baking class; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=OTHER; name=Baking class at a local culinary school; reason=Concrete event the user attended; useful as object of later attended_event claim.
- mention=local culinary school; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Local culinary school; reason=Institution mentioned as the place of the baking class; no proper name given.
- mention=strawberry shortcake; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=CONCEPT; name=Strawberry shortcake; reason=Reusable baking project/dessert concept repeatedly discussed.
- mention=farmer's market; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=LOCATION; name=Farmer's market; reason=Place/source where the user plans to get fresh strawberries.
- mention=whipping cream; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<6>; type=PRODUCT; name=Whipping cream; reason=Reusable food product/ingredient discussed as part of the recipe.
- mention=sourdough bread; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<7>; type=CONCEPT; name=Sourdough bread; reason=Reusable baking concept the user has been experimenting with.
- mention=my sourdough starter; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<8>; type=PRODUCT; name=User's sourdough starter; reason=First-person possessive identifies a concrete personal baking item that may be the object of future claims.
- mention=sourdough shortcake; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<9>; type=CONCEPT; name=Sourdough shortcake; reason=Reusable baking project concept discussed repeatedly.
- mention=flour; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<10>; type=PRODUCT; name=Flour; reason=General reusable baking ingredient category discussed as something the user experiments with.
- mention=bread flour; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<11>; type=PRODUCT; name=Bread flour; reason=Specific reusable ingredient the user has used and plans to try first.
- mention=oat flour; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<12>; type=PRODUCT; name=Oat flour; reason=Specific reusable ingredient the user has used for pancakes and waffles.
- mention=cookies; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<13>; type=CONCEPT; name=Cookies; reason=Baked good mentioned as result of user's bread flour experiment; reusable enough for later observation claim.
- mention=pancakes; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<14>; type=CONCEPT; name=Pancakes; reason=Baked/cooked food the user makes with oat flour; possible object of later claim.
- mention=waffles; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<15>; type=CONCEPT; name=Waffles; reason=Food the user makes with oat flour; possible object of later claim.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<16>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=user|strawberry shortcake|sourdough starter|sourdough shortcake|bread flour|oat flour|local culinary school|farmer's market
        text_queries=baking lately strawberries strawberry shortcake sourdough shortcake bread flour oat flour|baking class local culinary school yesterday strawberries|cookies with bread flour chewier oat flour pancakes waffles nutty flavor
        predicate_hints=prefers|has_experience_with|attended_event|metric_observation

        TARGET_MESSAGE source data:

LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2022/03/21 (Mon) 14:57
Transcript:
user: I'm looking for some inspiration for my next baking project. I've been really into baking lately.
assistant: What type of baking projects are you interested in tackling next? Are you looking for something sweet, savory, or perhaps something specific like bread, pastries, or cakes?

If you're open to suggestions, I can offer a few ideas to get you started:

1. **Flavored Cakes**: Consider experimenting with unique flavor combinations like lemon-rosemary, orange-almond, or chocolate-chipotle.
2. **Artisanal Breads**: Try your hand at making a crusty sourdough, a hearty whole-grain boule, or a flavorful focaccia.
3. **French Pastries**: Delve into the world of croissants, pain au chocolat, or eclairs. These classic treats require some skill, but the end result is well worth the effort.
4. **Seasonal Treats**: Create something inspired by the current season. For example, make a pumpkin spice cake in the fall, a citrusy tart in the winter, or a fresh berry pie in the summer.
5. **International Inspirations**: Explore baking traditions from around the world, such as Japanese mochi, Italian panettone, or Mexican conchas.
6. **Show-Stopping Desserts**: Go big with a multi-layer cake, a dramatic tart, or an elaborate dessert table with a variety of sweet treats.

Which of these ideas resonates with you, or do you have something else in mind? I'm happy to help you brainstorm and provide tips or recipe suggestions to get you started!
user: I've been obsessed with strawberries lately, especially after that amazing baking class I took at a local culinary school yesterday. I was thinking of making a strawberry shortcake. Do you have any tips on how to make the perfect shortcake?
assistant: That sounds like a wonderful experience! It's great that you're feeling inspired to keep baking and exploring new recipes. Strawberry shortcake is a classic! It's a perfect way to showcase fresh strawberries, and with a few tips, you can create a truly unforgettable dessert. Here are some secrets to making the perfect shortcake:

1. **Use the right flour**: Look for a high-protein flour, such as bread flour or all-purpose flour with a high protein content (around 12-13%). This will help the shortcake hold its structure and provide a tender crumb.
2. **Don't overmix**: Mix the dough just until the ingredients come together in a shaggy mass. Overmixing can lead to a dense, tough shortcake.
3. **Use cold ingredients**: Cold butter, cold cream, and cold eggs will help create a flaky, tender shortcake.
4. **Don't overbake**: Shortcake should be lightly golden and still tender in the center. Overbaking will make it dry and crumbly.
5. **Use the right sugar**: Granulated sugar is traditional, but you can also try using turbinado or Demerara sugar for a slightly caramel-like flavor.
6. **Add a touch of salt**: A pinch of salt will balance the sweetness and enhance the flavors of the strawberries and cream.
7. **Let it rest**: Allow the dough to rest for at least 30 minutes to allow the gluten to relax, making the shortcake easier to work with.
8. **Don't over-sweeten the strawberries**: Let the natural sweetness of the strawberries shine by only adding a touch of sugar to the sliced strawberries. You can also try using a mixture of granulated and confectioner's sugar for a more nuanced flavor.

Here's a simple recipe to get you started:

**Strawberry Shortcake Recipe**

Ingredients:

* 2 cups (250g) high-protein flour
* 1/4 cup (50g) granulated sugar
* 1/2 teaspoon salt
* 1/2 cup (115g) cold unsalted butter, cut into small pieces
* 3/4 cup (180ml) heavy cream, chilled
* 1 large egg, chilled
* 2 cups sliced strawberries, plus more for serving
* Confectioner's sugar, for dusting

Instructions:

1. Preheat your oven to 375°F (190°C). Line a baking sheet with parchment paper.
2. In a large bowl, whisk together the flour, sugar, and salt. Add the cold butter and use a pastry blender or your fingers to work it into the flour mixture until it resembles coarse crumbs.
3. In a small bowl, whisk together the heavy cream and egg until well combined. Pour the cream mixture over the flour mixture and gently fold until the dough comes together in a shaggy mass.
4. Turn the dough out onto a lightly floured surface and gently knead it a few times until it comes together.
5. Roll the dough out to about 1 inch (2.5 cm) thickness. Use a biscuit cutter or the rim of a glass to cut out shortcakes. Gather the scraps, re-roll, and cut out more shortcakes.
6. Place the shortcakes on the prepared baking sheet, leaving about 1 inch (2.5 cm) of space between each.
7. Brush the tops with a little extra heavy cream and sprinkle with granulated sugar.
8. Bake for 18-20 minutes, or until lightly golden. Allow the shortcakes to cool on a wire rack.
9. Split the shortcakes in half horizontally using a serrated knife.
10. Spoon sliced strawberries over the bottom half of each shortcake, followed by a dollop of whipped cream. Top with the top half of the shortcake and dust with confectioner's sugar.

Tips for assembling the shortcake:

* Make sure the shortcakes are completely cool before assembling to prevent them from becoming soggy
user: I'm thinking of using fresh strawberries from the farmer's market for my shortcake. I've been buying them by the container lately and snacking on them all day. They're so sweet and juicy! Do you have any recommendations for whipping cream? I want it to be light and fluffy, but still hold its shape.
assistant: Fresh strawberries from the farmer's market are the perfect choice for your shortcake! They're sure to add natural sweetness and flavor to your dessert.

Now, about that whipped cream... Whipping cream can be a bit finicky, but with a few tips, you'll be on your way to creating light, fluffy, and stable whipped cream:

1. **Chill the bowl and beaters**: Chill the bowl and beaters in the freezer for at least 10-15 minutes before whipping the cream. This will help the cream to whip up faster and to a lighter consistency.
2. **Use heavy cream**: Heavy cream (36% fat) is the best choice for whipping, as it contains more fat than whole milk or half-and-half. This will give you a richer, more stable whipped cream.
3. **Start with cold cream**: Make sure the cream is well-chilled before whipping. You can even refrigerate it overnight if you're planning to whip it the next day.
4. **Don't over-beat**: Whip the cream until it reaches the soft peak stage (when the cream forms soft, rounded peaks that still collapse slightly when the beaters are lifted). Over-beating will result in stiff, butter-like whipped cream.
5. **Add sugar gradually**: Add granulated sugar gradually, whipping well after each addition. This will help to dissolve the sugar evenly and prevent graininess.
6. **Use a touch of acidity**: Adding a pinch of salt or a squeeze of fresh lemon juice will help to stabilize the whipped cream and prevent it from weeping or becoming too soft.
7. **Don't over-whip**: Stop whipping once the cream has reached the desired consistency. Over-whipping will cause the cream to become too stiff and separate.

Here's a simple recipe for whipped cream:

**Whipped Cream Recipe**

Ingredients:

* 1 cup (240ml) heavy cream, chilled
* 2 tablespoons granulated sugar
* Pinch of salt
* 1/2 teaspoon vanilla extract (optional)

Instructions:

1. In a chilled bowl, beat the heavy cream with an electric mixer on medium-high speed until soft peaks form.
2. Gradually add the granulated sugar, whipping well after each addition.
3. Add the salt and vanilla extract (if using), and whip until combined.
4. Continue whipping until the cream reaches the desired consistency, stopping to scrape down the sides of the bowl as needed.
5. Use immediately, or refrigerate for up to 2 hours before using.

Tips for assembling the shortcake:

* Whip the cream just before assembling the shortcake to ensure it's at its lightest and fluffiest.
* Dollop the whipped cream onto the shortcake gently, using a spatula or spoon to create a smooth, rounded peak.
* You can also pipe the whipped cream onto the shortcake using a piping bag and tip, if desired.

With these tips, you'll be on your way to creating a show-stopping strawberry shortcake with light, fluffy, and stable whipped cream!
user: That's great advice on whipping cream! I've been experimenting with sourdough bread lately, and I was thinking of using some of my sourdough starter to make a sourdough shortcake instead of regular shortcake. Do you think that would work?
assistant: What a fascinating idea! Using sourdough starter to make a sourdough shortcake is a brilliant concept. The tangy, slightly sour flavor of the sourdough would complement the sweetness of the strawberries and whipped cream perfectly.

In theory, you can definitely use your sourdough starter to make a sourdough shortcake. However, keep in mind that sourdough starters are more finicky than traditional yeast, and you'll need to adjust the recipe and technique accordingly.

Here are some tips to help you create a successful sourdough shortcake:

1. **Adjust the recipe**: Sourdough starters are more acidic and have a higher water content than commercial yeast, so you'll need to adjust the recipe to compensate. You may need to add more flour, reduce the liquid content, or adjust the salt levels.
2. **Use a mature starter**: Make sure your sourdough starter is mature and active before using it in the recipe. A mature starter will have a more consistent fermentation rate, which is crucial for a successful sourdough shortcake.
3. **Longer proofing time**: Sourdough starters take longer to ferment than commercial yeast, so be prepared to proof the dough for a longer period, potentially 2-3 hours or even overnight.
4. **Gentle mixing**: Mix the dough gently to avoid developing the gluten, which can lead to a dense, tough shortcake.
5. **Lower oven temperature**: Sourdough breads often benefit from a lower oven temperature to prevent over-browning. You may need to reduce the oven temperature to 350°F (175°C) or even 325°F (165°C) to achieve the right crust color and texture.
6. **Monitor the texture**: Keep an eye on the shortcake's texture during the proofing and baking process. Sourdough can be more delicate, so you may need to adjust the baking time to prevent overcooking.

To get you started, here's a rough outline of a sourdough shortcake recipe:

**Sourdough Shortcake Recipe (Experimental)**

Ingredients:

* 1 cup (120g) sourdough starter (at 100% hydration)
* 2 cups (250g) bread flour
* 1/2 cup (60g) granulated sugar
* 1/2 teaspoon salt
* 1/4 cup (60ml) cold unsalted butter, cut into small pieces
* 1 large egg, beaten
* 1 teaspoon vanilla extract (optional)

Instructions:

1. Mix the sourdough starter, flour, sugar, and salt in a bowl until just combined.
2. Add the cold butter and mix until the dough comes together in a shaggy mass.
3. Knead the dough gently for 5-10 minutes until it becomes smooth and elastic.
4. Proof the dough at room temperature (around 75°F/24°C) for 2-3 hours, or until it has doubled in size.
5. Preheat your oven to 350°F (175°C).
6. Punch down the dough and shape into shortcakes.
7. Place the shortcakes on a baking sheet lined with parchment paper, leaving about 1 inch (2.5 cm) of space between each.
8. Brush the tops with beaten egg and sprinkle with granulated sugar.
9. Bake for 20-25 minutes, or until the shortcakes are golden brown and cooked through.
10. Allow the shortcakes to cool on a wire rack before assembling with strawberries and whipped cream.

Remember, this is an experimental recipe, and you may need to adjust it based on your sourdough starter's behavior and the shortcake's texture. Be prepared to make adjustments and take notes for future attempts.

I'm excited to hear how your sourdough shortcake turns out!
user: I'm thinking of trying a different type of flour for my sourdough shortcake.  I've been experimenting with different types of flour lately, and I've been loving the results. I made a batch of cookies with bread flour, and they turned out chewier than usual. I've also been using oat flour to make pancakes and waffles, and it gives them a nice nutty flavor. Do you think I could use oat flour or bread flour for my sourdough shortcake?
assistant: You're a flour adventurer! I love it! Exploring different types of flour can indeed lead to exciting results, and it's great that you're experimenting with various options.

Now, about using oat flour or bread flour for your sourdough shortcake:

**Oat Flour:**

Oat flour can add a delightful nutty flavor and a slightly denser texture to your shortcake. However, keep in mind that oat flour is a coarser, whole-grain flour that can affect the overall structure and rise of your shortcake. Oat flour also has a lower gluten content than all-purpose flour, which might impact the shortcake's texture and crumb.

If you decide to use oat flour, you may need to:

* Increase the liquid content in the recipe to compensate for the coarser texture.
* Adjust the proofing time, as oat flour can take longer to ferment.
* Be prepared for a denser, more rustic shortcake with a nutty flavor.

**Bread Flour:**

Bread flour, on the other hand, is a high-protein flour that can add strength and structure to your shortcake. It's a great choice if you want a more tender, chewy crumb. However, bread flour can also make the shortcake more dense and heavier than all-purpose flour.

If you decide to use bread flour, you may need to:

* Reduce the liquid content in the recipe, as bread flour can absorb more liquid.
* Monitor the proofing time, as bread flour can ferment more quickly.
* Be prepared for a more dense, chewy shortcake with a slightly heavier texture.

**Tips for using alternative flours:**

1. Start with a small batch to test the recipe and adjust as needed.
2. Be prepared to adjust the liquid content, proofing time, and temperature based on the flour's properties.
3. Consider blending the alternative flour with all-purpose flour to create a hybrid flour that balances the characteristics of both.
4. Keep an eye on the shortcake's texture and adjust the recipe accordingly. You may need to add more liquid, adjust the mixing time, or try a different proofing schedule.

Remember, experimenting with different flours is all about trial and error. Don't be discouraged if the first batch doesn't turn out as expected. Take notes, adjust, and try again!

Which flour do you think you'll try first? Oat flour or bread flour?
user: I think I'll try the bread flour first. I've had good results with it in the past, and I'm curious to see how it will affect the texture of the shortcake. I'll make sure to monitor the proofing time and adjust the liquid content as needed. Thanks for the tips and encouragement!
assistant: Bread flour is a great choice, and I'm sure it will add a lovely texture to your shortcake. Remember to keep an eye on the dough's development and adjust the recipe as needed. With bread flour, you might need to adjust the liquid content, but also keep an eye on the proofing time, as it can ferment more quickly.

I'm excited to hear about your experiment! Don't hesitate to reach out if you have any more questions or need further guidance. And most importantly, have fun and enjoy the process!

Remember, baking is all about experimentation and learning. It's okay to try new things and make mistakes – it's all part of the process. The most important thing is that you're having fun and enjoying the journey.

Good luck, and I hope your sourdough shortcake turns out amazing!

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Transcript:
user: I'm looking for some inspiration for my next baking project. I've been really into baking lately.
assistant: What type of baking projects are you interested in tackling next? Are you looking for something sweet, savory, or perhaps something specific like bread, pastries, or cakes?

If you're open to suggestions, I can offer a few ideas to get you started:

1. **Flavored Cakes**: Consider experimenting with unique flavor combinations like lemon-rosemary, orange-almond, or chocolate-chipotle.
2. **Artisanal Breads**: Try your hand at making a crusty sourdough, a hearty whole-grain boule, or a flavorful focaccia.
3. **French Pastries**: Delve into the world of croissants, pain au chocolat, or eclairs. These classic treats require some skill, but the end result is well worth the effort.
4. **Seasonal Treats**: Create something inspired by the current season. For example, make a pumpkin spice cake in the fall, a citrusy tart in the winter, or a fresh berry pie in the summer.
5. **International Inspirations**: Explore baking traditions from around the world, such as Japanese mochi, Italian panettone, or Mexican conchas.
6. **Show-Stopping Desserts**: Go big with a multi-layer cake, a dramatic tart, or an elaborate dessert table with a variety of sweet treats.

Which of these ideas resonates with you, or do you have something else in mind? I'm happy to help you brainstorm and provide tips or recipe suggestions to get you started!
user: I've been obsessed with strawberries lately, especially after that amazing baking class I took at a local culinary school yesterday. I was thinking of making a strawberry shortcake. Do you have any tips on how to make the perfect shortcake?
assistant: That sounds like a wonderful experience! It's great that you're feeling inspired to keep baking and exploring new recipes. Strawberry shortcake is a classic! It's a perfect way to showcase fresh strawberries, and with a few tips, you can create a truly unforgettable dessert. Here are some secrets to making the perfect shortcake:

1. **Use the right flour**: Look for a high-protein flour, such as bread flour or all-purpose flour with a high protein content (around 12-13%). This will help the shortcake hold its structure and provide a tender crumb.
2. **Don't overmix**: Mix the dough just until the ingredients come together in a shaggy mass. Overmixing can lead to a dense, tough shortcake.
3. **Use cold ingredients**: Cold butter, cold cream, and cold eggs will help create a flaky, tender shortcake.
4. **Don't overbake**: Shortcake should be lightly golden and still tender in the center. Overbaking will make it dry and crumbly.
5. **Use the right sugar**: Granulated sugar is traditional, but you can also try using turbinado or Demerara sugar for a slightly caramel-like flavor.
6. **Add a touch of salt**: A pinch of salt will balance the sweetness and enhance the flavors of the strawberries and cream.
7. **Let it rest**: Allow the dough to rest for at least 30 minutes to allow the gluten to relax, making the shortcake easier to work with.
8. **Don't over-sweeten the strawberries**: Let the natural sweetness of the strawberries shine by only adding a touch of sugar to the sliced strawberries. You can also try using a mixture of granulated and confectioner's sugar for a more nuanced flavor.

Here's a simple recipe to get you started:

**Strawberry Shortcake Recipe**

Ingredients:

* 2 cups (250g) high-protein flour
* 1/4 cup (50g) granulated sugar
* 1/2 teaspoon salt
* 1/2 cup (115g) cold unsalted butter, cut into small pieces
* 3/4 cup (180ml) heavy cream, chilled
* 1 large egg, chilled
* 2 cups sliced strawberries, plus more for serving
* Confectioner's sugar, for dusting

Instructions:

1. Preheat your oven to 375°F (190°C). Line a baking sheet with parchment paper.
2. In a large bowl, whisk together the flour, sugar, and salt. Add the cold butter and use a pastry blender or your fingers to work it into the flour mixture until it resembles coarse crumbs.
3. In a small bowl, whisk together the heavy cream and egg until well combined. Pour the cream mixture over the flour mixture and gently fold until the dough comes together in a shaggy mass.
4. Turn the dough out onto a lightly floured surface and gently knead it a few times until it comes together.
5. Roll the dough out to about 1 inch (2.5 cm) thickness. Use a biscuit cutter or the rim of a glass to cut out shortcakes. Gather the scraps, re-roll, and cut out more shortcakes.
6. Place the shortcakes on the prepared baking sheet, leaving about 1 inch (2.5 cm) of space between each.
7. Brush the tops with a little extra heavy cream and sprinkle with granulated sugar.
8. Bake for 18-20 minutes, or until lightly golden. Allow the shortcakes to cool on a wire rack.
9. Split the shortcakes in half horizontally using a serrated knife.
10. Spoon sliced strawberries over the bottom half of each shortcake, followed by a dollop of whipped cream. Top with the top half of the shortcake and dust with confectioner's sugar.

Tips for assembling the shortcake:

* Make sure the shortcakes are completely cool before assembling to prevent them from becoming soggy
user: I'm thinking of using fresh strawberries from the farmer's market for my shortcake. I've been buying them by the container lately and snacking on them all day. They're so sweet and juicy! Do you have any recommendations for whipping cream? I want it to be light and fluffy, but still hold its shape.
assistant: Fresh strawberries from the farmer's market are the perfect choice for your shortcake! They're sure to add natural sweetness and flavor to your dessert.

Now, about that whipped cream... Whipping cream can be a bit finicky, but with a few tips, you'll be on your way to creating light, fluffy, and stable whipped cream:

1. **Chill the bowl and beaters**: Chill the bowl and beaters in the freezer for at least 10-15 minutes before whipping the cream. This will help the cream to whip up faster and to a lighter consistency.
2. **Use heavy cream**: Heavy cream (36% fat) is the best choice for whipping, as it contains more fat than whole milk or half-and-half. This will give you a richer, more stable whipped cream.
3. **Start with cold cream**: Make sure the cream is well-chilled before whipping. You can even refrigerate it overnight if you're planning to whip it the next day.
4. **Don't over-beat**: Whip the cream until it reaches the soft peak stage (when the cream forms soft, rounded peaks that still collapse slightly when the beaters are lifted). Over-beating will result in stiff, butter-like whipped cream.
5. **Add sugar gradually**: Add granulated sugar gradually, whipping well after each addition. This will help to dissolve the sugar evenly and prevent graininess.
6. **Use a touch of acidity**: Adding a pinch of salt or a squeeze of fresh lemon juice will help to stabilize the whipped cream and prevent it from weeping or becoming too soft.
7. **Don't over-whip**: Stop whipping once the cream has reached the desired consistency. Over-whipping will cause the cream to become too stiff and separate.

Here's a simple recipe for whipped cream:

**Whipped Cream Recipe**

Ingredients:

* 1 cup (240ml) heavy cream, chilled
* 2 tablespoons granulated sugar
* Pinch of salt
* 1/2 teaspoon vanilla extract (optional)

Instructions:

1. In a chilled bowl, beat the heavy cream with an electric mixer on medium-high speed until soft peaks form.
2. Gradually add the granulated sugar, whipping well after each addition.
3. Add the salt and vanilla extract (if using), and whip until combined.
4. Continue whipping until the cream reaches the desired consistency, stopping to scrape down the sides of the bowl as needed.
5. Use immediately, or refrigerate for up to 2 hours before using.

Tips for assembling the shortcake:

* Whip the cream just before assembling the shortcake to ensure it's at its lightest and fluffiest.
* Dollop the whipped cream onto the shortcake gently, using a spatula or spoon to create a smooth, rounded peak.
* You can also pipe the whipped cream onto the shortcake using a piping bag and tip, if desired.

With these tips, you'll be on your way to creating a show-stopping strawberry shortcake with light, fluffy, and stable whipped cream!
user: That's great advice on whipping cream! I've been experimenting with sourdough bread lately, and I was thinking of using some of my sourdough starter to make a sourdough shortcake instead of regular shortcake. Do you think that would work?
assistant: What a fascinating idea! Using sourdough starter to make a sourdough shortcake is a brilliant concept. The tangy, slightly sour flavor of the sourdough would complement the sweetness of the strawberries and whipped cream perfectly.

In theory, you can definitely use your sourdough starter to make a sourdough shortcake. However, keep in mind that sourdough starters are more finicky than traditional yeast, and you'll need to adjust the recipe and technique accordingly.

Here are some tips to help you create a successful sourdough shortcake:

1. **Adjust the recipe**: Sourdough starters are more acidic and have a higher water content than commercial yeast, so you'll need to adjust the recipe to compensate. You may need to add more flour, reduce the liquid content, or adjust the salt levels.
2. **Use a mature starter**: Make sure your sourdough starter is mature and active before using it in the recipe. A mature starter will have a more consistent fermentation rate, which is crucial for a successful sourdough shortcake.
3. **Longer proofing time**: Sourdough starters take longer to ferment than commercial yeast, so be prepared to proof the dough for a longer period, potentially 2-3 hours or even overnight.
4. **Gentle mixing**: Mix the dough gently to avoid developing the gluten, which can lead to a dense, tough shortcake.
5. **Lower oven temperature**: Sourdough breads often benefit from a lower oven temperature to prevent over-browning. You may need to reduce the oven temperature to 350°F (175°C) or even 325°F (165°C) to achieve the right crust color and texture.
6. **Monitor the texture**: Keep an eye on the shortcake's texture during the proofing and baking process. Sourdough can be more delicate, so you may need to adjust the baking time to prevent overcooking.

To get you started, here's a rough outline of a sourdough shortcake recipe:

**Sourdough Shortcake Recipe (Experimental)**

Ingredients:

* 1 cup (120g) sourdough starter (at 100% hydration)
* 2 cups (250g) bread flour
* 1/2 cup (60g) granulated sugar
* 1/2 teaspoon salt
* 1/4 cup (60ml) cold unsalted butter, cut into small pieces
* 1 large egg, beaten
* 1 teaspoon vanilla extract (optional)

Instructions:

1. Mix the sourdough starter, flour, sugar, and salt in a bowl until just combined.
2. Add the cold butter and mix until the dough comes together in a shaggy mass.
3. Knead the dough gently for 5-10 minutes until it becomes smooth and elastic.
4. Proof the dough at room temperature (around 75°F/24°C) for 2-3 hours, or until it has doubled in size.
5. Preheat your oven to 350°F (175°C).
6. Punch down the dough and shape into shortcakes.
7. Place the shortcakes on a baking sheet lined with parchment paper, leaving about 1 inch (2.5 cm) of space between each.
8. Brush the tops with beaten egg and sprinkle with granulated sugar.
9. Bake for 20-25 minutes, or until the shortcakes are golden brown and cooked through.
10. Allow the shortcakes to cool on a wire rack before assembling with strawberries and whipped cream.

Remember, this is an experimental recipe, and you may need to adjust it based on your sourdough starter's behavior and the shortcake's texture. Be prepared to make adjustments and take notes for future attempts.

I'm excited to hear how your sourdough shortcake turns out!
user: I'm thinking of trying a different type of flour for my sourdough shortcake.  I've been experimenting with different types of flour lately, and I've been loving the results. I made a batch of cookies with bread flour, and they turned out chewier than usual. I've also been using oat flour to make pancakes and waffles, and it gives them a nice nutty flavor. Do you think I could use oat flour or bread flour for my sourdough shortcake?
assistant: You're a flour adventurer! I love it! Exploring different types of flour can indeed lead to exciting results, and it's great that you're experimenting with various options.

Now, about using oat flour or bread flour for your sourdough shortcake:

**Oat Flour:**

Oat flour can add a delightful nutty flavor and a slightly denser texture to your shortcake. However, keep in mind that oat flour is a coarser, whole-grain flour that can affect the overall structure and rise of your shortcake. Oat flour also has a lower gluten content than all-purpose flour, which might impact the shortcake's texture and crumb.

If you decide to use oat flour, you may need to:

* Increase the liquid content in the recipe to compensate for the coarser texture.
* Adjust the proofing time, as oat flour can take longer to ferment.
* Be prepared for a denser, more rustic shortcake with a nutty flavor.

**Bread Flour:**

Bread flour, on the other hand, is a high-protein flour that can add strength and structure to your shortcake. It's a great choice if you want a more tender, chewy crumb. However, bread flour can also make the shortcake more dense and heavier than all-purpose flour.

If you decide to use bread flour, you may need to:

* Reduce the liquid content in the recipe, as bread flour can absorb more liquid.
* Monitor the proofing time, as bread flour can ferment more quickly.
* Be prepared for a more dense, chewy shortcake with a slightly heavier texture.

**Tips for using alternative flours:**

1. Start with a small batch to test the recipe and adjust as needed.
2. Be prepared to adjust the liquid content, proofing time, and temperature based on the flour's properties.
3. Consider blending the alternative flour with all-purpose flour to create a hybrid flour that balances the characteristics of both.
4. Keep an eye on the shortcake's texture and adjust the recipe accordingly. You may need to add more liquid, adjust the mixing time, or try a different proofing schedule.

Remember, experimenting with different flours is all about trial and error. Don't be discouraged if the first batch doesn't turn out as expected. Take notes, adjust, and try again!

Which flour do you think you'll try first? Oat flour or bread flour?
user: I think I'll try the bread flour first. I've had good results with it in the past, and I'm curious to see how it will affect the texture of the shortcake. I'll make sure to monitor the proofing time and adjust the liquid content as needed. Thanks for the tips and encouragement!
assistant: Bread flour is a great choice, and I'm sure it will add a lovely texture to your shortcake. Remember to keep an eye on the dough's development and adjust the recipe as needed. With bread flour, you might need to adjust the liquid content, but also keep an eye on the proofing time, as it can ferment more quickly.

I'm excited to hear about your experiment! Don't hesitate to reach out if you have any more questions or need further guidance. And most importantly, have fun and enjoy the process!

Remember, baking is all about experimentation and learning. It's okay to try new things and make mistakes – it's all part of the process. The most important thing is that you're having fun and enjoying the journey.

Good luck, and I hope your sourdough shortcake turns out amazing!
